title: Kog Laneformer 2B: The Latency-First Model Behind Kog Inference Engine
gist: Paris-based startup Kog has open-sourced Laneformer 2B, a 2.3B-parameter coding model co-designed from scratch with its inference engine to maximize single-request decoding speed using a novel 'Delayed Tensor Parallelism' mechanism that hides inter-GPU communication costs. The model achieves 3,000 tokens/second per request on 8x AMD MI300X and 2,100 tokens/second on 8x NVIDIA H200 — claimed to be the fastest publicly demonstrated single-request result for a 2B-class model — while reaching competitive coding benchmark scores (45.1% HumanEval+, 51.6% MBPP+). This release demonstrates that architecture-runtime co-design can achieve extreme inference speeds at startup resource scale using open-source data and infrastructure.

insights.claim: Kog's Laneformer 2B is a 2.3B-parameter model trained from scratch using a novel 'Delayed Tensor Parallelism' (DTP) architecture that hides inter-GPU communication costs inherent in standard Tensor Parallelism, treating decoding speed as a primary design objective rather than a post-training serving concern.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: DTP represents a fundamentally different design philosophy — co-designing model architecture with inference runtime — which, if validated at larger scales, could shift how AI labs approach model development and create new competitive advantages.
insights.quote: for the fastest single-request inference, architecture and runtime should be designed together. Laneformer is our first model trained from scratch to explore that co-design point.

insights.claim: Standard Tensor Parallelism forces inter-GPU synchronization at every layer boundary, making the communication overhead especially costly at batch-size-one decoding; Delayed Tensor Parallelism delays this synchronization, but naive implementations degrade model quality, requiring minimal architectural changes to restore training stability.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The difficulty of implementing DTP without quality loss explains why most inference providers have not pursued this approach, creating a potential moat for Kog if the technique proves durable.
insights.quote: doing this naively leads to subpar model quality: once hidden states are no longer synchronized at the usual boundaries, model quality starts to drop off sharply and finding architectural ideas becomes necessary for training stability.

insights.claim: Laneformer 2B uses an 8-lane structure with a delay of 2 to perfectly hide TP communication overhead, combined with Grouped-Query Attention using 32 query heads and 16 key/value heads sharded evenly across 8 lanes, and Sliding Window Attention for 10 of 15 model layers.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The specific architecture choices (8 lanes, GQA 32/16, SWA on 10/15 layers) are calibrated for the 8-GPU inference target, meaning the speed benefits require the Kog Inference Engine — a deliberate integration point that ties model to runtime.
insights.quote: The strongest architectural change is the new 8-lane system to support DTP. To perfectly hide the TP communication overhead, we calibrated the delay to 2.

insights.claim: At low batch sizes, decode speed is bounded by GPU memory bandwidth rather than FLOPs, because each generated token requires streaming model weights and KV cache from GPU memory to compute units; Laneformer's architecture is explicitly sized to maximize tokens-per-second within this memory-bandwidth-bound regime.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The memory-bandwidth-bound insight at low batch size is the architectural first principle behind Laneformer; investors evaluating AI inference hardware should understand that single-request latency is a distinct optimization target from throughput.
insights.quote: At low batch sizes, decode speed is not just a FLOPs problem. A lot of time goes into moving weights, synchronizing kernels, and paying communication costs layer after layer.

insights.claim: Laneformer 2B reaches 3,000 output tokens per second per request on 8x AMD MI300X and 2,100 output tokens per second per request on 8x NVIDIA H200 using FP16, batch size 1, and no speculative decoding — claimed to be the fastest publicly demonstrated single-request decoding result for any 2B-class model on standard datacenter GPUs.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: A 3,000 TPS single-request result at 2B scale sets a new public benchmark for real-time AI agent applications where response latency directly impacts user experience, and validates AMD MI300X as competitive with H200 for this use case.
insights.quote: Laneformer 2B reaches 3,000 output tokens/s/request on 8× AMD MI300X and 2,100 output tokens/s/request on 8× NVIDIA H200, using FP16, batch size 1, and no speculative decoding.

insights.claim: A single inference pass of Laneformer 2B takes less than 0.3 seconds, making it practical to sample multiple candidate code solutions (pass@N for N=2, 4, 8, 16) to trade a small latency increment for higher coding accuracy.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: Sub-0.3s latency per pass enables stochastic decoding strategies (best-of-N) to be practical at low additional cost, unlocking reliability improvements for autonomous coding agents without requiring larger models.
insights.quote: As a single pass usually takes less than 0.3 seconds, sampling multiple candidate solutions becomes a practical way to trade a small amount of latency for higher coding accuracy.
insights.locator: Evaluation section, Comparison with similar-size models

insights.claim: Laneformer 2B reaches 45.1% HumanEval+ and 51.6% MBPP+ in greedy decoding, described as 'extremely competitive for a model of its size' relative to other public models in the 2B parameter range.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: Benchmark-competitive coding performance at 2B scale while achieving extreme inference speed suggests the speed-quality tradeoff may be less severe than conventional wisdom suggests for targeted domains.
insights.quote: Laneformer 2B reaches 45.1% HumanEval+ and 51.6% MBPP+ in greedy decoding.

insights.claim: Kog trained Laneformer 2B on approximately 4 trillion pre-training tokens followed by approximately 2 trillion code and reasoning-heavy mid-training tokens and approximately 210 million post-training tokens, using 192 H100 GPUs (24 nodes of 8) over about 21 days on European infrastructure from Scaleway and ADASTRA.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The training compute footprint (192 H100-days over 21 days) is within reach of well-funded startups, validating that competitive small model development does not require hyperscaler resources.
insights.quote: 24 nodes with 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs each, for 192 H100 GPUs... About 21 days of training.

insights.claim: Kog gathered more than 20TB of high-quality filtered open-source training data leveraging NVIDIA's Nemotron dataset work to avoid the costly data mixture search, then applied a two-phase recipe: a broad generalist Phase 1 followed by a heavily code-and-reasoning-focused Phase 2 mid-training phase.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: Leveraging NVIDIA's open Nemotron datasets allowed a startup to access frontier-quality training data without proprietary data collection costs, demonstrating the compounding value of open data releases.
insights.quote: Thanks to the current open ecosystem, and particularly NVIDIA's work on Nemotron, we were able to gather more than 20TB of high-quality filtered open-source data and avoid the long and costly data mixture search.

insights.claim: Phase 2 mid-training on code and reasoning-heavy data improved HumanEval+ scores by more than 10 points over the Phase 1 base model post-trained for coding, validating the aggressive phase-2 data shift, though CORE tracking confirms it came at a non-negligible cost to general capabilities.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The 10+ point coding improvement from domain-focused mid-training quantifies the value of data mixture specialization and validates the deliberate general-capability tradeoff that domain-specific AI products can accept.
insights.quote: Phase 2 did improve our model's coding performance by more than 10 points across HumanEval+ tests.

insights.claim: Kog's pre-training achieved approximately 17,000 tokens per second per GPU during training on 192 H100 GPUs using TorchTitan with FSDP, AdamW, and WSD learning-rate schedule at mixed FP32/BF16 precision.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: Publishing training throughput metrics enables cost benchmarking for other small-model training efforts and validates the efficiency of the TorchTitan + FSDP stack for custom architectures.
insights.quote: We also spent quite a lot of time precisely tuning... to reach about 17k tokens/s/GPU during pre-training.

insights.claim: Kog is releasing Laneformer 2B weights, custom HuggingFace architecture code, and full training recipe (pre-training, mid-training, post-training) under Apache License 2.0, positioning the model as both a usable checkpoint and a research artifact to advance latency-first model design.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: Open-sourcing the full training recipe — not just weights — is a strong community-building play that could attract researchers and companies interested in latency-first design, building an ecosystem around Kog's inference engine.
insights.quote: We believe open source is the best way to make this kind of systems and architecture work useful, inspectable, and extensible by the broader community.

insights.claim: The standard HuggingFace Transformers-compatible Laneformer 2B checkpoint does not provide the main latency benefits — achieving 3,000 TPS requires the Kog Inference Engine and its DTP-aware execution path — creating a clear distinction between the open-source artifact and Kog's commercial inference product.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The open-source weight release drives ecosystem adoption and benchmarking while keeping the high-value inference speed advantage proprietary within the Kog Inference Engine, a classic open-core business model.
insights.quote: the released Hugging Face checkpoint is useful as a standard model, but the main latency benefits require the Kog Inference Engine and the DTP-aware execution path.

insights.claim: Kog is a Paris-based AI infrastructure startup building a real-time inference engine for AI agents targeting 3,000 tokens per second single-request performance on standard datacenter GPUs, positioning itself at the intersection of LLM architecture research and low-level GPU engineering.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: Kog occupies an emerging niche between model providers and inference optimization vendors, which could prove valuable as AI agent applications demand sub-second response times at scale.
insights.quote: Kog is a Paris-based AI infrastructure startup building a real-time inference engine for AI agents with innovative low-level GPU engineering and LLM architecture research.

insights.claim: Laneformer 2B has a 4,096-token context length as a deliberate design constraint reflecting latency targets, with long-context extension described as currently in progress — limiting its applicability for RAG and long-document workloads in its current release.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The short context window reflects architectural tradeoffs made for inference speed; investors in AI agent infrastructure should weigh latency-first models against the growing importance of long-context for complex agent reasoning tasks.
insights.quote: Its 4,096-token context length and sliding-window attention choices reflect our latency target. Long-context extension is currently in progress.

themes.theme: Implications for AI Hardware and Inference Economics

insights.claim: At batch-size-one decoding, GPU memory bandwidth rather than compute FLOPs determines maximum tokens per second, meaning model architecture decisions (weight volume, KV cache size, layer count) directly set the theoretical ceiling on single-request inference speed independent of kernel optimization.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: This memory-bandwidth framing is directly relevant to AI hardware evaluation: chips with high memory bandwidth (MI300X, H200) are specifically advantaged for real-time agent use cases even if their raw FLOP counts are similar.
insights.quote: the maximum theoretical tokens per second depends strongly on how much data must be read per forward pass.

insights.claim: AMD MI300X achieves higher single-request throughput than NVIDIA H200 for Laneformer 2B (3,000 vs 2,100 TPS), suggesting AMD's memory bandwidth advantage translates directly to better performance in the memory-bandwidth-bound batch-size-one inference regime.
insights.speaker: Morgan Giraud
insights.so_what: The MI300X outperforming H200 for low-batch inference challenges the conventional narrative of NVIDIA's dominance and supports AMD's positioning in inference-optimized deployments.
insights.quote: Laneformer 2B reaches 3,000 output tokens/s/request on 8× AMD MI300X and 2,100 output tokens/s/request on 8× NVIDIA H200
